lesson.title: Normal Distribution
lesson.body: The Normal (Gaussian) distribution N(μ, σ²) has the bell-shaped PDF f(x) = (1/√(2πσ²))·exp(-(x-μ)²/(2σ²)). It is fully characterized by its mean μ and variance σ². The standardization Z = (X-μ)/σ gives the standard normal N(0,1). The 68-95-99.7 rule states that roughly 68%, 95%, and 99.7% of values fall within 1, 2, and 3 standard deviations of the mean.

rooms.question: What percentage of observations fall within μ ± 2σ for a normal distribution?
rooms.explanation: The 68-95-99.7 rule: ~68% within 1σ, ~95% within 2σ, ~99.7% within 3σ of the mean.

rooms.question: If X ~ N(50, 100), what is P(X ≤ 60)?
rooms.explanation: Standardize: Z = (60−50)/10 = 1. P(Z ≤ 1) ≈ 0.8413 from standard normal tables.

rooms.question: What is the PDF of the standard normal N(0,1)?
rooms.explanation: The standard normal PDF is φ(z) = (1/√(2π))·e^{-z²/2}. The factor 1/√(2π) ensures it integrates to 1.

rooms.question: Order the steps to find P(a < X < b) for X ~ N(μ, σ²):
items: Look up Φ(Z₂) and Φ(Z₁) in z-table
State the answer as the probability
rooms.explanation: Standardize both endpoints, look up their CDF values in the standard normal table, then subtract.

rooms.question: If X ~ N(μ, σ²) and Y = aX + b, what is the distribution of Y?
rooms.explanation: Linear transformations of normal RVs remain normal. The mean transforms as aμ+b and variance scales by a².

boss.name: The Gaussian Ghost
boss.taunt: Everything converges to me. You cannot escape the bell curve.

questions.question: The Central Limit Theorem states that the sample mean X̄ of n i.i.d. RVs with mean μ and variance σ² has approximately which distribution for large n?
questions.explanation: CLT: X̄ ~ N(μ, σ²/n) approximately. The standard error is σ/√n.

questions.question: For X ~ N(0,1), what is P(|X| > 1.96)?
questions.explanation: P(Z > 1.96) ≈ 0.025, and by symmetry P(|Z| > 1.96) = 2·0.025 = 0.05. This is the basis for 95% confidence intervals.

questions.question: The moment generating function of X ~ N(μ, σ²) is:
questions.explanation: The MGF of N(μ, σ²) is M(t) = exp(μt + σ²t²/2). This uniquely characterizes the normal distribution and is useful for proving CLT.
